fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that static analysis tool we've been talking about for the Angular codebase. Basically devs keep shipping components and directives with inconsistent naming, sketchy event bindings, and gnarly template logic that nobody catches in review. It's causing real pain in prod — we had that incident last sprint where a custom event shadowed a browser event and broke the checkout flow on Safari.

The tool should read source code, run one of the named checks against it, and spit out a structured report so CI can fail on violations. A few of the checks also need to be able to auto-fix the source when asked — similar to how we handled the auto-correct flow in that i18n annotation pass we did a while back, just apply the same sentinel-block pattern.

Input comes in over stdin as a JSON blob, output goes to stdout. Line/column positions in findings need to be consistent so golden-file diffing works. The whole thing should be modular — each check lives independently so we can add more later without touching existing ones. Need to handle bad input gracefully too, no raw stack traces leaking out. We're thinking Python but open to discussion. Can someone scope this out and get it running against the test cases in the repo? The test files lay out the exact scenarios we care about.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: all line and column positions in findings are 0-based, so the first line is line 0 and the first character on a line is character 0. Also for the component/directive selector checks, the span should point at the selector value only, not the whole `selector: '...'` bit. So if you’ve got `selector: 'foo-bar'`, the range starts on the `f` and ends right before the closing quote. The concrete example to follow is `start_character=12, end_character=21` for `selector: 'foo-bar'`.

On fixes, when `apply_fix` is true and there’s at least one finding with a mechanical fix, we still print all the failure blocks first and then append the rewritten source using the sentinel block format. The exact shape is `fixed_source<<EOF\n<rewritten source>\nEOF\n`, so literally a line `fixed_source<<EOF`, then the rewritten source text, then a line `EOF`. Same pattern as the earlier sentinel-block flow, and that exact contract is what’s shown in the feature7_async_boolean_expression.json test cases.

A couple config details too: `max_cost` defaults to 5 if nothing is set, which is why the first test case with a 3-operator expression passes by default. For that complexity check, the cost is based on logical operators (`||`, `&&`) and comparison operators (`===`, `!==`, `==`, `!=`) inside the `*ngIf` expression. If the cost goes over the limit, the message needs to be exactly `The condition complexity (cost '<N>') exceeded the defined limit (cost '<max>'). The conditional expression should be moved into the component.`

For the modularity piece, each check should hang off the same abstract interface, like `run(source, config) -> list[Finding]`, and the engine just routes by check name to the right implementation. Adding a new check should just mean registering a new class, without changing existing checks or the engine core, and the adapter layer should only know about the abstract `Finding` type, not concrete check classes.

Last couple bits on input/output behavior: `prefixes` can be either a single string like `"sg"` or a list like `["fo", "mg", "sg"]`, and if it’s a list then matching any one of them counts as a pass. For `pipe_name` messages, if there are multiple prefixes they should be joined with commas and no spaces. And for command handling, bad JSON must return exactly `error=invalid_json`, a non-`lint` action must return exactly `error=unsupported_action`, and any internal failure must return exactly `error=execution_error`. We should never leak exception names or stack traces to stdout.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def analyze(source: str, check_name: str, config: dict | None = None, apply_fix: bool = False) -> AnalysisReport

trigger_keywords: component selector prefix check message
what message does component selector produce
selector diagnostic message format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a missing prefix on a component selector the message is: `The selector of the component "<ClassName>" should have prefix "<prefix>" (https://angular.io/styleguide#style-02-07)`. For a wrong case style on a directive selector: `The selector of the directive "<ClassName>" should be named <case_style> (https://angular.io/styleguide#style-02-06)`. For a missing prefix on a directive selector: `The selector of the directive "<ClassName>" should have prefix "<prefix>" (https://angular.io/styleguide#style-02-08)`.

trigger_keywords: pipe name failure message
what does the pipe_name diagnostic say
pipe check message format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pipe_name failure message is: `The name of the Pipe decorator of class <ClassName> should be named <case_style> with prefix <prefix(es)>, however its value is "<value>"`. When multiple prefixes are configured they are joined with commas and no spaces, e.g. `fo,mg,sg`. The span covers the full `name: 'value'` property expression, not just the string literal.

trigger_keywords: input alias failure message
what message does input_alias produce
aliased input diagnostic text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The input_alias failure message is: `In the class "<ClassName>", the directive input property "<propertyName>" should not be renamed. However, you should use an alias when the directive name is also an input property, and the directive name doesn't describe the property. In this last case, you can disable this rule with \`tslint:disable-next-line:no-input-rename\`.` The span covers the entire @Input(...) decorated property declaration.

trigger_keywords: output standard event message
what does output_standard_event report
native event collision diagnostic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output_standard_event failure message is: `In the class "<ClassName>", the output property "<propertyName>" should not be named or renamed after a standard event`. The public event name checked is the alias if one is provided, otherwise the property name. The span covers the entire @Output(...) decorated property declaration line.

trigger_keywords: ngFor trackBy missing message
repeated_template_rendering diagnostic
what does repeated template rendering report
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The repeated_template_rendering failure message is: `Missing trackBy function in ngFor directive`. The span covers the *ngFor attribute value (the microsyntax expression), not the full element tag. The check looks for the absence of a `trackBy:` clause in the ngFor microsyntax string.

trigger_keywords: async boolean negation message
what does async_boolean_expression report
async pipe fix message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two patterns are detected: (1) negation `!(foo | async)` → message: `Async pipes can not be negated, use (observable | async) === false instead`, fixed by replacing `!(foo | async)` with `(foo | async) === false`; (2) loose equality `(foo | async) == false` → message: `Async pipes must use strict equality \`===\` when comparing with \`false\``, fixed by replacing `==` with `===`. Both produce a fixed_source when apply_fix is true.

trigger_keywords: i18n localization check behavior
localized_template_text what does it check
check-id requirement for i18n
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When config requirement is `check-id`, the check validates that i18n attributes have a non-empty custom identifier after the `@@` separator. An empty id like `foo@@` triggers two findings (both pointing to the same span of the i18n attribute value). A valid id like `test@@foo` produces failure_count=0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'lint'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: We had that incident last sprint where a custom event shadowed a browser event and broke the checkout flow
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 (output_standard_event check): output bindings whose public name matches a known standard browser event name (e.g. `change`) must be flagged — see public_test_cases/feature5_output_standard_event.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please refer to the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected behavior — each feature's JSON file contains input/output pairs that define the precise wire contract. If something is still unclear after reviewing those, let me know which specific check or output format you're unsure about.
